title: Framework Coverage Gap Analysis
description: Identifies coverage gaps across frameworks for each risk domain. A gap means the framework does not address or only minimally addresses the risk domain.

criticalGaps.description: No provisions for AI environmental impact, energy consumption, or carbon footprint.
No provisions for deepfakes, synthetic media, or AI-generated misinformation.
No provisions for training data copyright or output IP ownership.

partialGaps.description: General security mentioned but no adversarial-specific provisions (data poisoning, adversarial attacks, prompt injection).
Fairness principle addresses broadly but lacks specific technical requirements for bias testing.

recommendations: Add environmental impact assessment requirements to align with UNESCO and OECD
Include deepfake/synthetic media disclosure requirements following EU AI Act Art. 50 model
Add adversarial robustness testing requirements aligned with NIST MEASURE-2.7
Consider IP/copyright provisions as GenAI adoption grows in Malaysia

partialGaps.description: Art. 27 fundamental rights impact assessment exists but limited compared to NIST MAP-5 societal impact scope.
Only addresses energy efficiency for systemic risk GPAI (Art. 55), not all AI systems.

recommendations: Extend environmental provisions beyond systemic risk GPAI
Broaden societal impact assessment to cover workforce displacement

criticalGaps.description: No specific provisions for deepfakes, synthetic media, or AI manipulation.
No provisions for training data IP or output copyright.

partialGaps.description: MANAGE-2.3 addresses unknown risks but lacks depth on adversarial testing compared to EU Art. 55.
MEASURE-2.10 privacy assessment exists but less comprehensive than EU Art. 5 prohibitions.

recommendations: Develop companion profile for GenAI misinformation risks
Expand adversarial testing guidance in future RMF updates
Add IP/copyright considerations for GenAI use cases

criticalGaps.description: No environmental impact provisions.
No deepfake or synthetic media provisions.
No IP/copyright provisions.

partialGaps.description: A.3.2 addresses roles but lacks EU Art. 14 level of human override/interrupt requirements.

recommendations: Future revisions should add environmental impact controls
Consider GenAI-specific controls for misinformation in annex updates
Add IP/copyright controls as GenAI certification demand grows

criticalGaps.description: Not addressed.
Not addressed.
Not addressed.

partialGaps.description: Internal governance principle exists but less detailed than EU provider/deployer obligations.

recommendations: Agentic AI addendum could be model for other emerging risk areas
Consider environmental impact provisions for data centre-heavy Singapore

criticalGaps.description: Not specifically addressed.
Not addressed.

partialGaps.description: Principle 1 mentions environmental challenges broadly but lacks specific provisions.

recommendations: 2024 update improved environmental language but still lacks specifics
Future updates should address GenAI misinformation risks

criticalGaps.description: Not addressed.

partialGaps.description: Safety and Security principle is broad, lacks adversarial-specific guidance.

recommendations: Strongest environmental coverage among soft-law instruments
Could strengthen adversarial security provisions
IP/copyright increasingly relevant as member states grapple with GenAI

criticalGaps.description: No provisions for AI environmental impact, energy consumption, or sustainability. Regulation focuses entirely on content safety and social stability.
No provisions for explaining AI decisions to affected individuals. Transparency requirements focus on content labeling rather than decision-making explainability.
No comprehensive human rights impact assessment framework. Content restrictions are framed around state security and social stability rather than individual rights protection.

partialGaps.description: Implicit risk differentiation through security assessment triggers (Art. 17) but no formal risk classification tiers or systematic risk-based approach.
Art. 4 prohibits discrimination and Art. 8 requires data diversity, but lacks specific fairness metrics, bias testing requirements, or third-party bias auditing.
Art. 7 requires respect for intellectual property rights in training data but lacks detailed provisions for output ownership or fair use in AI training.

recommendations: Add environmental impact assessment requirements for large-scale GenAI training and deployment
Develop explainability requirements for AI decisions that affect individuals
Establish formal risk classification tiers aligned with international approaches
Strengthen bias testing requirements with specific fairness metrics and independent auditing
Clarify IP/copyright provisions for GenAI outputs and training data use

criticalGaps.description: No provisions for AI environmental impact or sustainability. Framework focuses on governance principles without addressing energy consumption or carbon footprint.
No specific provisions for deepfakes, synthetic media, or AI-generated misinformation. Online Safety Act addresses some content harms but not AI-specific generation.
Not addressed in the framework. UK IPO consulted on text and data mining but no AI-specific copyright provisions have been adopted.

partialGaps.description: Decentralized approach risks inconsistent application across sector regulators. DRCF coordination exists but no binding cross-sector standards.
No centralized risk classification. Each regulator determines risk thresholds independently, creating potential gaps for cross-sector AI applications.
Initially non-statutory — principles rely on existing regulatory powers which may not cover all AI-specific risks. Government reviewing whether statutory duty needed.

recommendations: Consider environmental impact provisions especially for foundation model training in UK data centres
Address deepfake and synthetic content risks either through framework update or Online Safety Act amendments
Establish cross-sector consistency mechanism beyond DRCF coordination for AI risk classification
Evaluate whether statutory footing is needed for binding cross-sector AI requirements
Develop IP/copyright guidance for GenAI following UK IPO consultation outcomes

criticalGaps.description: Revoked prior safety benchmarks and red-team testing mandates from EO 14110. No binding safety testing requirements for AI systems.
No transparency or disclosure requirements for AI use. Prior AI disclosure mandates revoked. No federal AI labeling or watermarking requirements.
No bias testing or fairness requirements. Section 2 mentions freedom from ideological bias but provides no technical fairness standards or testing mandates.
No human oversight requirements. Prior human-in-the-loop mandates for federal AI use revoked.
No AI-specific data privacy provisions. No federal comprehensive privacy law. Prior AI data governance guidance revoked.
No environmental provisions. Focus on accelerating AI infrastructure including data centres without environmental safeguards.
Not addressed. Copyright Office has issued guidance but EO does not address training data IP or output ownership.

partialGaps.description: Action plan covers national security risks but no systematic risk management framework for commercial AI. NIST AI RMF remains voluntary.
No AI-specific enforcement mechanism at federal level. FTC has taken some enforcement actions under existing consumer protection authority.

recommendations: Reinstate or develop safety testing requirements for high-risk AI systems, potentially through NIST standards
Establish federal AI transparency and disclosure requirements, particularly for consumer-facing AI
Develop comprehensive federal AI privacy legislation
Consider environmental impact provisions for large-scale AI training and data centre expansion
Establish AI-specific enforcement authority or clarify existing agency jurisdiction over AI harms
Address IP/copyright framework for GenAI at the federal level

criticalGaps.description: No binding AI legislation. AIDA died in Parliament in January 2025. Only voluntary code of conduct remains, with no penalties or enforcement mechanism.
No provisions for AI environmental impact or sustainability in the Voluntary Code.
No provisions for deepfakes, synthetic media, or AI-generated misinformation.
Not addressed. No AI-specific copyright or IP provisions.
No enforcement mechanism. AIDA had proposed AI and Data Commissioner with enforcement powers and criminal liability for reckless AI deployment. All enforcement proposals died with the bill.

partialGaps.description: No risk classification framework. AIDA had proposed high-impact AI system classification but did not pass.
PIPEDA applies to personal data in AI systems but no AI-specific data governance requirements. CPPA (proposed PIPEDA replacement) also died with Bill C-27.
Voluntary Code mentions human oversight as a principle but provides no specific mechanisms, thresholds, or requirements.
Fairness included as a Voluntary Code principle but no specific bias testing, fairness metrics, or audit requirements.

recommendations: Develop new AI legislation following the 'light, tight, right' approach signaled by government
Establish binding requirements at minimum for high-impact AI systems
Add environmental impact provisions aligned with Canada's climate commitments
Develop AI-specific data governance requirements to complement existing PIPEDA framework
Address deepfake and misinformation risks through dedicated provisions
Establish enforcement mechanism — consider AI Commissioner model from proposed AIDA
Develop IP/copyright guidance for GenAI training data and outputs

crossCuttingGaps.description: Only the EU AI Act (Art. 53 for GPAI) addresses training data copyright. This is a critical gap across nearly all frameworks as GenAI adoption accelerates.
Only the EU AI Act comprehensively addresses deepfakes (Art. 50) and manipulation (Art. 5). Most frameworks predate the GenAI explosion.
Only UNESCO and NIST provide meaningful environmental coverage. Most frameworks ignore AI's growing energy footprint.
